Scalp and Skull Injuries
Source Basis: RadPrimer + STATdx master source for the scalp/skull injury article and image examples. Core Radiology support is limited to the captured cross-checks in core_evidence.txt: pediatric abuse fracture specificity, bone scintigraphy insensitivity for skull fractures, and general CT-versus-MRI trauma-fracture context.
Key distinctions
  • Cephalohematoma: subperiosteal, focal, and bounded by sutures.
  • Subgaleal hematoma: under the scalp aponeurosis, extensive, crosses sutures/midline, and can be life-threatening in infants.
  • Growing skull fracture: progressive widening/unhealing fracture from dural-arachnoid tear and CSF pulsations; CT shows rounded/scalloped lucency and MR can show CSF in the defect.
  • Fracture morphology: linear, depressed, elevated, and diastatic patterns should be named before searching for complications.
  • Complications: search for vascular injury, dural/arachnoid laceration with CSF leak, cranial nerve injury, pneumocephalus, EDH/SDH/SAH/contusion, and rare growing skull fracture.
Core cross-check
  • Isolated linear skull fracture is nonspecific for abuse.
  • Complex skull fractures that are depressed, multiple, bilateral, or cross sutures are suspicious for abuse.
  • Bone scintigraphy is insensitive for skull fractures.
  • CT is preferred for trauma fracture assessment when imaging is indicated; MRI is mainly for selected complications/soft-tissue or ligament questions.

column1: Patient with delayed scalp deformity E9P4C2H7M1QX
column3:
Axial NECT in a patient with a "growing" skull fracture shows a widened fracture with scalloped margins .
Axial T2 MR in the same patient shows a lobulated CSF collection filling the "growing" fracture. CSF pulsations have widened and scalloped the calvarium. "Growing" skull fractures are difficult to detect in the acute stage and are often identified on follow-up imaging as a progressively widening, unhealing fracture. On CT, they are lucent lesions with rounded, scalloped margins.

column7: CT head. Based on the image, name 3 key differentials and give one best discriminator for each.
column9:

Scalp laceration -> scalp discontinuity, air, or foreign material rather than a smooth suture-limited blood collection.

column1: Patient after severe head trauma J3K7M2T8Q5RC [DD2]
column3:
Axial NECT shows a large subgaleal hematoma crossing the sagittal suture. Hyperdense vertex hematoma also crosses the midline, suggesting that the blood is in the epidural space.
Axial bone CT shows a diastatic fracture of the sagittal suture . A very large subgaleal hematoma extends around the entire circumference of the skull. The superior sagittal sinus is torn, and the intracranial blood seen on soft tissue windows was a vertex venous epidural hematoma.
column7: Bone CT head. Based on the image, name 4 key differentials and give one best discriminator for each.
column9:
Pattern differs because: this drill separates traumatic calvarial lucencies from normal skull mimics rather than separating scalp hematoma layers.
Skull fracture -> sharply marginated traumatic lucency; source emphasizes fractures are rarely present without overlying scalp swelling.
Normal suture -> anatomic suture line without traumatic soft-tissue swelling.
Vascular groove -> normal vascular channel mimic; absence of scalp swelling favors this normal mimic.
Wormian bone -> accessory sutural bone; absence of traumatic swelling and typical sutural location favor mimic over fracture.

column11: Why does a cephalohematoma stop at sutures while a subgaleal hematoma can cross sutures and midline?
column12:
Cephalohematoma is subperiosteal: blood is trapped deep to periosteum, and periosteum is anchored at cranial sutures, so the collection remains focal and suture-limited.
Subgaleal hematoma is under the scalp aponeurosis/galea rather than under periosteum. That potential space is not bounded by sutures, so blood can spread broadly, cross midline, and extend around the calvarium.
Imaging pivot: a suture-limited scalp mass favors cephalohematoma; a diffuse collection crossing sutures/midline favors subgaleal hematoma and can be life-threatening in infants.

column11: Why can a growing skull fracture become progressively widened and scalloped on CT/MR?
column12:
The source frames growing skull fracture as posttraumatic leptomeningeal cyst formation. A dural/arachnoid laceration permits CSF-containing leptomeninges/CSF to occupy the fracture gap.
CSF pulsations repeatedly transmit pressure across the defect, widening and scalloping the calvarium over time.
Imaging pivot: CT shows a progressively widening, unhealing lucent defect with rounded/scalloped margins, while T2 MR can show a lobulated CSF collection filling the defect.

column13: What is the trap, and what safety check prevents it?
column14:
Trap: calling a normal calvarial lucency a skull fracture, or dismissing a true fracture as a normal line.
Safety check: look for overlying scalp swelling/soft-tissue injury and trace the line for expected anatomy.
Key discriminator: skull fractures are rarely present without overlying scalp swelling; absence of swelling strongly favors sutures, vascular grooves, venous lakes, arachnoid granulations, or wormian bones.
Why the error occurs: normal skull channels and sutural anatomy can visually mimic sharply marginated fracture lines on bone windows.

column3: Axial graphic depicts different basilar skull fractures crossing the petrous apex and clivus as well as extending into the jugular foramen and carotid canal . The potential for vascular injury in such fractures is high.
column13: What is the pitfall here, and what safety check prevents it?
column14:
Trap: treating a skull base fracture near vascular channels as an isolated bone injury.
Safety check: evaluate whether the fracture involves the temporal bone, sphenoid/clivus, carotid canal, jugular foramen, or dural venous sinus; consider CTA/MRA when these high-risk pathways are involved.
Key discriminator: fractures crossing the carotid canal, jugular foramen, or dural venous sinus region carry higher vascular or venous sinus injury risk.
Why the error occurs: bone-window fracture detection can distract from the vascular-channel complication search.

column15: What defines a linear skull fracture in this source?
column16:
A linear skull fracture is a sharply marginated fracture line.
Exam pivot: An isolated linear skull fracture is nonspecific for child abuse and can occur with accidental trauma.

Source Basis: RadPrimer + STATdx master source for the scalp/skull injury article and image examples. Core Radiology support is limited to the captured cross-checks in core_evidence.txt: pediatric abuse fracture specificity, bone scintigraphy insensitivity for skull fractures, and general CT-versus-MRI trauma-fracture context.
Key distinctions
  • Cephalohematoma: subperiosteal, focal, and bounded by sutures.
  • Subgaleal hematoma: under the scalp aponeurosis, extensive, crosses sutures/midline, and can be life-threatening in infants.
  • Growing skull fracture: progressive widening/unhealing fracture from dural-arachnoid tear and CSF pulsations; CT shows rounded/scalloped lucency and MR can show CSF in the defect.
  • Fracture morphology: linear, depressed, elevated, and diastatic patterns should be named before searching for complications.
  • Complications: search for vascular injury, dural/arachnoid laceration with CSF leak, cranial nerve injury, pneumocephalus, EDH/SDH/SAH/contusion, and rare growing skull fracture.
Core cross-check
  • Isolated linear skull fracture is nonspecific for abuse.
  • Complex skull fractures that are depressed, multiple, bilateral, or cross sutures are suspicious for abuse.
  • Bone scintigraphy is insensitive for skull fractures.
  • CT is preferred for trauma fracture assessment when imaging is indicated; MRI is mainly for selected complications/soft-tissue or ligament questions.

column15: What defines a depressed skull fracture in this source?
column16:
A depressed skull fracture has fragments that implode inward.
Exam pivot: depressed fracture morphology suggests higher-force focal impact and is one of the complex patterns that raises abuse concern in the Core cross-check when seen in the appropriate pediatric context.

column15: What defines an elevated skull fracture in this source?
column16:
An elevated skull fracture has a segment that rotates or lifts away from the calvarium, sometimes described as hinged away from the skull.
Exam pivot: do not call every displaced fragment depressed; elevated fracture is a distinct morphology shown in the source images.

column15: What defines a diastatic skull fracture in this source?
column16:
A diastatic skull fracture widens a suture or synchondrosis.
Exam pivot: diastasis can allow skull-fracture/extraaxial collections to cross expected boundaries and should prompt careful review for venous sinus injury or vertex EDH when near the sagittal suture.

column15: What complications should be actively searched for after skull fracture?
column16:
Vascular injury.
Dural/arachnoid laceration with possible CSF leak.
Cranial nerve injury.
Rare growing skull fracture.
Exam pivot: skull fracture reporting should include complication search, not just fracture morphology.

column15: What is the key risk/outcome pivot for large subgaleal hematoma in infants?
column16:
Large or expanding subgaleal hematomas in infants can cause life-threatening blood loss.
Exam pivot: diffuse scalp blood crossing sutures in an infant is not just a local scalp finding; it can be hemodynamically dangerous.
